title: The Changing Economic World: Nigeria
examTip: Nigeria is an NEE, not an LIC — always use this classification. For TNC questions, you need both advantages (jobs, tax revenue) AND disadvantages (oil spills, profit repatriation) with specific evidence.
introTips: Nigeria is an NEE — West Africa's largest economy; don't call it an LIC.
Shell's oil operations are the go-to TNC example — know both benefits and the environmental damage.
The north-south divide within Nigeria is an important inequality point for 6-mark answers.
intro: Nigeria is a country of extraordinary contrasts: Africa's most populous nation, its largest economy, home to vast oil reserves and the world's second-largest film industry — yet also a place where millions live on under $2 a day, where oil spills have poisoned farmland for generations, and where the gap between north and south, rich and poor, is vast. Nigeria's story shows both what rapid development can achieve and what it can cost.

keyPoints.heading: Nigeria in context
keyPoints.content: Nigeria is located in West Africa and is the continent's most populous country with over 220 million people. It is also Africa's largest economy, with a GDP of approximately $440 billion. Nigeria is classified as an NEE (Newly Emerging Economy) — transitioning from a primarily agriculture-based economy towards industry and services. Nigeria has abundant natural resources: oil (Niger Delta — 9th largest reserves in the world), natural gas, coal, tin, iron ore, cocoa and palm oil. Its colonial history is significant: under British rule from 1861 to 1960, the British exploited groundnuts, palm oil and tin, and drew borders that cut across 250+ ethnic groups including the Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba and Igbo. These divisions have caused ongoing political tensions.
writeBullets: Location — West Africa; largest population in Africa (220m+)
Largest economy in Africa — GDP ~$440bn
NEE — Newly Emerging Economy; transitioning from agriculture to industry and services
Natural resources — oil (9th largest reserves), gas, coal, cocoa, palm oil
British colonial rule 1861–1960 — exploited resources, drew arbitrary borders
250+ ethnic groups — Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, Igbo — divisions persist
keyPoints.memTip: Nigeria = Africa's most populous + most populous = pressure. Lots of people, lots of resources, lots of inequality.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The colonial legacy is important context for understanding Nigeria's development challenges — ethnic tensions and unfair trade patterns both trace back to this period.

keyPoints.heading: Nigeria's changing economy
keyPoints.content: In the 1960s, Nigeria's economy was primarily agricultural (cocoa, groundnuts). Oil was discovered in 1956 and by the 1970s had become dominant. Today, oil accounts for over 90% of Nigeria's export earnings and about 70% of government revenue. However, oil employs only around 3% of the workforce because it is capital-intensive (machines, not people, do the work), and volatile oil prices have caused economic boom and bust cycles. Manufacturing is growing in Lagos: Unilever, Nestlé and Cadbury all have factories there. The services sector is expanding rapidly — banking is growing, and Nollywood (Nigeria's film industry) is the world's second-largest film industry by output after Bollywood, generating billions of dollars. A growing middle class — projected at 23 million households by 2030 — is driving consumer demand. Lagos is Sub-Saharan Africa's financial hub and a megacity.
writeBullets: Oil dominates — 90%+ of exports, 70% of government revenue since 1970s
Oil employs only 3% of workforce — capital-intensive, not labour-intensive
Volatile oil prices cause boom-bust economic cycles
Manufacturing growing — Lagos: Unilever, Nestlé, Cadbury factories
Nollywood — world's 2nd largest film industry by output; multi-billion dollar industry
Growing middle class — projected 23m households by 2030
Lagos — megacity and Sub-Saharan Africa's financial hub
keyPoints.memTip: Oil earns the money but creates few jobs — Nigeria needs to diversify beyond oil.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The contrast between oil's share of export revenue (90%) and employment (3%) is a powerful statistic to use in exam answers about uneven development.

keyPoints.heading: TNCs: Shell and others
keyPoints.content: TNCs (Transnational Corporations) play a major role in Nigeria's economy. Shell has operated oil extraction in the Niger Delta since 1958. Benefits of TNCs: government receives significant tax revenue and royalties; over 10,000 direct jobs created; infrastructure (roads, hospitals) was built near Shell facilities; technology transfer to local workers. Disadvantages: most profits are repatriated to the Netherlands and UK (Shell's home countries); relatively few local jobs given the industry's scale; environmental damage is severe. Over 50 years, an estimated 9–13 million barrels of oil have spilled in the Niger Delta. The Bodo Creek spill (2008–9) discharged around 500,000 barrels, contaminating 69km² of creeks and farmland. Other TNCs include Unilever (consumer goods — soaps, food) and Diageo (Guinness Nigeria). Their presence brings technology transfer but raises questions about whether benefits reach ordinary Nigerians or primarily benefit shareholders abroad.
writeBullets: Shell in Niger Delta since 1958 — major oil extractor; 10,000+ direct jobs
Government benefits — tax revenue and royalties from oil
Infrastructure built near Shell facilities — roads, hospitals
Profits repatriated to Netherlands/UK — most economic benefit leaves Nigeria
9–13m barrels estimated spilled over 50 years — severe pollution
Bodo Creek spill 2008–9 — 500,000 barrels; 69km² contaminated
Other TNCs — Unilever (soaps/food), Diageo (Guinness Nigeria)
keyPoints.memTip: Shell drills deep — profits go far away, pollution stays in Nigeria.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Bodo Creek spill is a specific, named example that lifts exam answers — learn the figures (500,000 barrels, 69km²).

keyPoints.heading: Aid, trade and investment
keyPoints.content: Nigeria receives various forms of international economic engagement. The UK (through FCDO, formerly DFID) has provided bilateral aid to Nigeria, though the amount has reduced in recent years; recent focus has been on governance and girls' education. Chinese investment is growing rapidly: China has committed approximately $23 billion to Nigerian infrastructure including roads, railways, and the Abuja light rail — in exchange for oil access. This raises concerns about Nigeria accumulating debt to China. The IMF has attached structural adjustment conditions to its support — including reducing government subsidies and privatising state companies — which have been controversial. Fairtrade cocoa schemes (e.g., Divine Chocolate) attempt to return more money to farmers. Nigeria is a member of ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States), a regional trade bloc aimed at reducing trade barriers and increasing economic integration across 15 West African nations.
writeBullets: UK aid (FCDO) — focused on governance and girls' education; reducing in recent years
Chinese investment — $23bn committed; roads, railways, Abuja light rail in exchange for oil
Risk of debt dependency on China — 'debt trap' concern
IMF structural adjustment — conditions: reduce subsidies, privatise state companies; controversial
Fairtrade cocoa — Divine Chocolate returns more money to farmers
ECOWAS — 15-nation West African trade bloc; reduces barriers, increases integration
keyPoints.memTip: China builds, UK funds schools, IMF sets conditions — three different types of international engagement.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Chinese investment is a nuanced topic — it brings infrastructure but may create debt dependency. Good for evaluation marks.

keyPoints.heading: Inequality and environmental issues
keyPoints.content: Nigeria's development is extremely uneven. The north (Hausa-Fulani Muslim majority) has lower literacy rates, more subsistence farming, and has been severely affected by the Boko Haram insurgency since 2009, which has killed over 100,000 people and displaced more than 2 million. The south (more development, oil wealth, Christian majority) has better infrastructure and higher incomes. Gender inequality persists: female literacy is around 55% versus 71% for males. The contrast between Lagos and rural north-east Nigeria is one of the starkest development divides within any single country. Niger Delta oil spills have caused catastrophic environmental damage to the Ogoniland region. The Ogoni people, led by activist Ken Saro-Wiwa, protested Shell's pollution — Saro-Wiwa was executed by the Abacha military government in 1995, causing international outrage. A UNEP report (2011) estimated that cleaning up Ogoniland would take 30 years and cost $1 billion.
writeBullets: North-south divide — north: lower literacy, subsistence farming, Boko Haram insurgency
Boko Haram — 100,000+ killed, 2m+ displaced since 2009 in north-east Nigeria
Gender inequality — female literacy 55% vs male 71%
Ogoniland — oil pollution devastated farmland and fishing; UNEP 2011: 30-year cleanup needed
Ken Saro-Wiwa — Ogoni activist protested Shell pollution; executed 1995; international outcry
Natural gas flaring — wastes energy, emits CO₂, causes health problems for nearby communities
UNEP Ogoniland report (2011) — estimated $1bn and 30 years to clean up
keyPoints.memTip: North struggles, south develops — and even in the oil-rich south, Ogoniland shows the environmental cost.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Ken Saro-Wiwa is a powerful human story linking environmental damage, corporate accountability and human rights — use it in 6-mark evaluation questions.

tables.title: Advantages and disadvantages of Shell's operations in Nigeria
rows: 10,000+ direct jobs; local engineers and workers trained
Only 3% of workforce employed in oil — capital-intensive; few jobs relative to investment
Tax revenue and royalties fund public services
Oil revenue volatile — boom-bust cycles destabilise government budgets
Roads, hospitals built near Shell facilities
Infrastructure mainly benefits oil operations, not wider communities
9–13m barrels estimated spilled in 50 years; Bodo Creek 2008–9: 500,000 barrels, 69km² contaminated
Most profits repatriated to Netherlands/UK — economic benefit leaves Nigeria

2.question: What was the environmental impact of the Bodo Creek oil spill in 2008–9?
options: Approximately 500,000 barrels were discharged, contaminating 69km² of creeks and farmland
Around 50,000 barrels were spilled, contaminating 6km² of mangrove forest
The spill caused an explosion at the refinery, destroying 30km of pipeline
The oil entered Lagos harbour, closing the port for six months
2.correctFeedback: Correct! The Bodo Creek spill was catastrophic — approximately 500,000 barrels contaminated 69km² of the Niger Delta. It's one of the most important specific examples of TNC environmental damage to know.
2.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The Bodo Creek spill (2008–9) released approximately 500,000 barrels of oil, contaminating 69km² of creeks, farmland and fishing grounds in the Niger Delta.

4.question: What did the UNEP Ogoniland report (2011) conclude about oil pollution in Nigeria?
options: That cleaning up Ogoniland would take approximately 30 years and cost $1 billion
That Shell had already begun a successful cleanup and the land would recover within 5 years
That the Nigerian government was solely responsible for the pollution, not Shell
That the pollution was exaggerated and most farmland was still productive
4.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The UNEP 2011 report on Ogoniland was damning — it estimated a 30-year cleanup costing $1 billion, highlighting just how severe the long-term damage from oil extraction has been.
4.wrongFeedback: That's not what UNEP found. The 2011 Ogoniland report concluded that cleanup would take approximately 30 years and cost around $1 billion — a devastating assessment of decades of oil pollution.

commonMistakes: Calling Nigeria an LIC — it is an NEE (Newly Emerging Economy) with Africa's largest GDP. Poverty is due to inequality and poor distribution of wealth, not a lack of development overall.
Saying oil 'employs most Nigerians' — oil provides 90% of export revenue but employs only about 3% of workers because it is capital-intensive (machines-led).
Forgetting to evaluate TNCs — always give both sides: jobs and infrastructure (benefits) AND profit repatriation and environmental damage (costs).
Not naming the Ogoni people or Ken Saro-Wiwa — these specific details push answers from Level 2 to Level 3 in extended writing.
Confusing Nigeria with Niger — Nigeria is the large NEE; Niger is a neighbouring LIC with very low development indicators. They are different countries.
examTips: For TNC questions, use the table structure: employment, income, infrastructure, environment, economic leakage — this ensures you cover advantages AND disadvantages systematically.
Quote the Bodo Creek spill figures (500,000 barrels, 69km²) and UNEP (30 years, $1bn cleanup) — specific evidence signals a top-band answer.
For inequality questions, use the north-south divide within Nigeria — Boko Haram in the north vs oil wealth in the south — and link to colonial history (arbitrary borders).
The Ken Saro-Wiwa case links environmental damage to human rights — powerful material for 'assess the impact of TNCs' 9-mark questions.
revisionCardBullets: Nigeria — West Africa; 220m+ people; Africa's largest economy ($440bn GDP)
NEE — Newly Emerging Economy; transitioning from agriculture to industry/services
Oil dominates — 90%+ exports, 70% government revenue; discovered 1956
Oil = only 3% of jobs — capital-intensive; creates boom-bust economic cycles
Nollywood — world's 2nd largest film industry by output
Shell in Niger Delta since 1958 — 10,000+ jobs, tax revenue, infrastructure
Shell: 9–13m barrels spilled in 50 years; Bodo Creek 2008–9 — 500,000 barrels, 69km²
Chinese investment — $23bn; roads, railways, Abuja light rail for oil access
North-south divide — Boko Haram (100,000+ killed) in north; oil wealth in south
Gender inequality — female literacy 55% vs male 71%
Ogoniland — devastating oil pollution; Ken Saro-Wiwa executed 1995
UNEP 2011 — Ogoniland cleanup: 30 years, $1 billion estimated cost
